Forms pyrochlore but cannot be made by conventional solid-state synthesis of Bi2O3 + HfO2; requires coprecipitation followed by calcination at 700-950°C; exhibits alpha/beta/gamma/delta polymorphs at ~400/550/900°C; delta polymorph is ideal cubic pyrochlore (Fd-3m); decomposes at ~1000°C into HfO2 + Bi1.94Hf0.06O3.03 (beta-Bi2O3 structure); Bi volatility precludes high-temp solid-state routes; ref: 10.1021/cm062864a (Henderson et al. 2007)

Forms pyrochlore but is metastable — cannot be made by conventional solid-state synthesis; requires coprecipitation from H2O2/NH3(aq) solutions of Ti with aqueous Bi(NO3)3; pure phase only obtainable within a few degrees of 470°C; decomposes to Bi4Ti3O12 + Bi2Ti4O11 above ~600°C; Li stabilization extends stability to 1100°C but changes composition; refs: 10.1016/S0022-4596(03)00378-5 (Hector & Wiggin 2004) and 10.1021/cm202154c (Esquivel-Elizondo et al. 2011)

Forms metastable pyrochlore-like phase — cannot be made by conventional solid-state synthesis; requires EDTA-chelation of equimolar Zr4+/Bi3+ followed by calcination at 750°C/3h in air; product contains minor beta-Bi2O3 impurity; phase is metastable and decomposes at higher temperatures into ZrO2 + Bi1.84Zr0.16O3.08 (beta-Bi2O3 structure); ref: 10.1021/acs.inorgchem.8b02258
